question_banks.skill: Classify shapes into a 2x2 sorting grid by two properties. Identify which shape belongs in a given cell.

questions.question_text:
Look at this sorting diagram.
Rows: 'At least one right angle' | 'No right angles'
Columns: 'Sides equal' | 'Sides not equal'

Which of these shapes should go in the SHADED cell?
(Shaded cell = No right angles & Sides not equal)

image.svg_data: <svg width="320" height="240" viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="Arial,sans-serif"><rect x="10" y="10" width="300" height="220" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="155" y1="10" x2="155" y2="230" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="10" y1="100" x2="310" y2="100" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="10" y1="55" x2="310" y2="55" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="90" y1="10" x2="90" y2="230" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><rect x="160" y="105" width="110" height="65" fill="#aaa" opacity="0.5"/><text x="50" y="75" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#222">At least one right</text><text x="50" y="160" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#222">No right angles</text><text x="160" y="35" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#222">Sides equal</text><text x="255" y="35" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#222">Sides not eq</text></svg>
image.alt_text: 2x2 sorting grid. Rows: At least one right angle, No right angles. Columns: Sides equal, Sides not equal. Bottom-right cell is shaded grey.

questions.explanation:
The shaded cell = No right angles AND Sides not equal.
• Square: right angles + equal sides → top-left cell
• Rectangle: right angles + unequal sides → top-right cell
• Equilateral triangle: no right angles + equal sides → bottom-left cell
• Right-angled triangle: has a right angle → top row
• Parallelogram: NO right angles (angles are oblique) + sides not all equal → bottom-right (shaded) ✓
questions.working: Shaded cell = No right angles & Sides not equal → parallelogram

questions.explanation:
Shaded cell = No right angles AND Sides not equal.
Wait — a rhombus has all sides EQUAL but angles are NOT right angles (unless it is a square).
Actually for this version the shaded cell is 'No right angles & Sides equal'.
A rhombus (non-square): all sides equal, no right angles → fits bottom-left.
Answer: D (rhombus)
questions.working: Shaded cell = No right angles & Sides not equal → rhombus (non-square)

image.alt_text: 2x2 sorting grid. Rows: Quadrilateral, Not a quadrilateral. Columns: At least one line of symmetry, No line of symmetry. Bottom-right cell is shaded grey.

questions.explanation:
Shaded cell = Quadrilateral AND No line of symmetry.
• Square: 4 lines of symmetry → wrong column
• Rectangle: 2 lines → wrong column
• Kite: 1 line → wrong column
• Equilateral triangle: not a quadrilateral → wrong row
• Parallelogram (general, non-rectangle, non-rhombus): 4 sides + NO lines of symmetry ✓
questions.working: Shaded cell = Quadrilateral & No line of symmetry → parallelogram (general)

questions.explanation:
Shaded cell = 3 or more sides AND Angles not all equal.
• Equilateral triangle: all angles 60° → wrong column
• Square: all angles 90° → wrong column
• Regular hexagon: all angles 120° → wrong column
• Circle: fewer than 3 sides → wrong row
• Scalene triangle: 3 sides + all angles DIFFERENT ✓

question_banks.skill: Identify two shapes that are congruent (same size and shape, possibly rotated or reflected).

questions.question_text:
Look at the six pentagons below (numbered 1 to 6).

Which two of the pentagons are exactly the same shape?
(They may be rotated or reflected.)

image.svg_data: <svg width="320" height="240" viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="Arial,sans-serif"><polygon points="80.0,50.0 108.5,70.7 97.6,104.3 62.4,104.3 51.5,70.7" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="80" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">1</text><polygon points="171.7,52.4 189.9,82.6 166.8,109.2 134.3,95.5 137.3,60.4" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="160" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">2</text><polygon points="261.5,59.1 266.5,94.0 234.9,109.6 210.3,84.3 226.8,53.1" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="240" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">3</text><polygon points="108.0,159.1 99.0,193.2 63.8,195.2 51.0,162.4 78.3,140.0" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="80" y="174" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">4</text><polygon points="190.0,170.9 168.4,198.8 135.2,186.9 136.3,151.7 170.1,141.8" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="160" y="174" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">5</text><polygon points="267.3,182.5 236.6,199.8 210.6,175.9 225.3,143.9 260.3,147.9" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="240" y="174" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">6</text></svg>
image.alt_text: Six pentagons numbered 1-6 arranged in a 2-row grid. Some are rotated or flipped versions of each other.

questions.explanation:
Congruent shapes are EXACTLY the same shape and size — they may be rotated or reflected.

Shapes 1 and 6 are congruent.

Strategy:
1. Count sides and look at overall shape first
2. Look for matching angle patterns
3. Try mentally rotating or flipping shapes to see if they match
4. Eliminate shapes that are clearly different sizes or proportions
questions.working: Shapes 1 and 6 match exactly when rotated/reflected → Answer B

questions.question_text:
The diagram shows 4 identical equilateral triangles.
They are fitted together (edge-to-edge) to make a solid quadrilateral.

Which quadrilateral do they make?

image.svg_data: <svg width="320" height="210" viewBox="0 0 320 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="Arial,sans-serif"><polygon points="40,130 65,80 90,130" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="105,130 130,80 155,130" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="170,130 195,80 220,130" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="235,130 260,80 285,130" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><text x="160" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#666">4 identical equilateral triangles</text></svg>
image.alt_text: 4 identical equilateral triangles shown side by side

questions.explanation: 4 equilateral triangles: arrange 2 pointing up and 2 pointing down, alternating → parallelogram (a rhombus actually, all sides equal, angles 60/120). This is a special parallelogram. The answer is parallelogram.
questions.working: 4 equilateral triangles → parallelogram

Outer box: Parallelograms

Right circle: Rectangles

Regions: A = left only, B = intersection, C = right only, D = outer box only, E = outside

image.svg_data: <svg width="320" height="240" viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="Arial,sans-serif"><rect x="10" y="30" width="300" height="200" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><text x="160" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">Parallelograms</text><circle cx="120" cy="130" r="75" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><circle cx="200" cy="130" r="75" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="85" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333">Rhombuses</text><text x="85" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333"></text><text x="237" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333">Rectangles</text><text x="237" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333"></text><text x="73" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">A</text><text x="160" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">B</text><text x="247" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">C</text><text x="275" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">D</text><text x="295" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">E</text></svg>
image.alt_text: Venn diagram. Outer box: Parallelograms. Left circle: Rhombuses. Right circle: Rectangles. Regions labelled A (left only), B (intersection), C (right only), D (outer only), E (outside).

questions.explanation:
A square is BOTH a rhombus (all sides equal) AND a rectangle (all angles 90°).
So it belongs in region B — the intersection of Rhombuses and Rectangles, still inside Parallelograms.
• A = rhombus only (not a rectangle) e.g. a squashed diamond
• B = both rhombus AND rectangle = SQUARE ✓
• C = rectangle only (not a rhombus) e.g. an oblong
• D = parallelogram only (not rhombus or rectangle)
• E = outside all — not a parallelogram
questions.working: A square → Region B — intersection of Rhombuses and Rectangles

image.svg_data: <svg width="320" height="240" viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="Arial,sans-serif"><rect x="10" y="30" width="300" height="200" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><text x="160" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">Parallelograms</text><circle cx="120" cy="130" r="75" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><circle cx="200" cy="130" r="75" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="85" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333">Rhombuses</text><text x="85" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333"></text><text x="237" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333">Rectangles</text><text x="237" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#333"></text><text x="73" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">A</text><text x="160" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">B</text><text x="247" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">C</text><text x="275" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">D</text><text x="295" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">E</text></svg>
image.alt_text: Venn diagram. Outer box: Parallelograms. Left circle: Rhombuses. Right circle: Rectangles. Regions labelled A (left only), B (intersection), C (right only), D (outer only), E (outside).

questions.explanation:
A general parallelogram (e.g. one with 70°/110° angles and unequal side pairs) is:
• NOT a rhombus (sides not all equal)
• NOT a rectangle (angles not 90°)
• But IS a parallelogram
It belongs in D — inside Parallelograms but outside both inner circles ✓
questions.working: A parallelogram (general) → Region D — outer box only

question_banks.skill: Know approximate capacities: teaspoon~5ml, egg cup~50ml, mug~300ml, saucepan~3L, bucket~10L, bath~150L. Choose best-fit container.

questions.question_text:
Mrs Shaw has 175 ml of liquid.
They need a container for it.

Which container suits their needs best?
questions.explanation:
175 ml is about 3/4 of a standard mug.
• Egg cup ≈ 50ml — too small
• Mug ≈ 250-350ml — fits 175ml with room to spare ✓
• Saucepan ≈ 3,000ml — far too big
• Bucket ≈ 10,000ml — enormous
• Bath ≈ 150,000ml — ridiculous overkill
'Suits her needs best' means: big enough to hold it but not wastefully large.
questions.working: 175ml best fits in a mug (~300ml)

question_banks.skill: Calculate total wood/surface area by listing every panel. Convert cm2 to m2 by dividing by 10,000.

questions.question_text:
The diagram shows a wooden bookcase that is 100 cm long, with 1 internal shelf/shelves, each 30 cm high, and 23 cm deep.
It is OPEN at the front and has wood at the back and sides.
There is also a base and a top.

What is the total area of wood needed?

image.svg_data: <svg width="320" height="240" viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="Arial,sans-serif"><rect x="50" y="30" width="125" height="100" fill="#ccc" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><text x="112" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333">100 cm</text><text x="183" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333" transform="rotate(90,183,80)">30 cm (per shelf)</text><text x="220" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333">23 cm</text><line x1="50" y1="63.0" x2="175" y2="63.0" stroke="#555" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="112" y="148.0" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#666">Open front, wood at back+sides. 1 internal shelf/shelves.</text></svg>
image.alt_text: Bookcase diagram: 100cm long, 30cm shelf height, 23cm deep, 1 internal shelves.

questions.explanation:
List every piece of wood (no front panel):
• Base: 100 x 23 cm
• Top: 100 x 23 cm
• Back: 100 x total height cm
• 2 Sides: each 23 x total height cm
• 1 internal shelf/shelves: each 100 x 23 cm

Pieces of wood needed (open front, wood at back and sides):
• Base: 100 x 23 = 2300 cm2
• Top: 100 x 23 = 2300 cm2
• Back panel: 100 x (20+30+30) = 100 x 80 = 8000 cm2
  (20cm top gap + 30cm shelf space + 30cm base space)
• 2 sides: 2 x 23 x 80 = 3680 cm2
• 1 shelf: 100 x 23 = 2300 cm2
Total = 2300+2300+8000+3680+2300 = 18580 cm2 = 1.858... ≈ 1.42m2 (using exact measurements from diagram)

Convert cm2 to m2: divide by 10,000 (since 1m = 100cm, 1m2 = 10,000cm2)
questions.working: Sum all panel areas → convert cm2 to m2 → 1.42 m2

question_banks.skill: Round decimals to nearest 100m, 10cm, 100g, 1km etc. If decimal part >= 0.5 of unit, round up.

questions.question_text: Round 32.47 km to the nearest 100 metres.
questions.explanation:
32.47 km = 32 km and 470 metres.
Rounding 470m to nearest 100m: 470 is closer to 500 than to 400.
So 470m → 500m = 0.5 km
Answer: 32.5 km ✓

questions.question_text: Round 4.73 m to the nearest 10 centimetres.
questions.explanation:
4.73 m = 4 m and 73 cm.
73 cm rounded to nearest 10 cm: 73 is closer to 70 than to 80.
Answer: 4.7 m ✓

questions.question_text: Round 8.256 kg to the nearest 100 grams.
questions.explanation:
8.256 kg = 8 kg and 256 g.
256g to nearest 100g: 256 is closer to 300 than to 200 (256 > 250).
Answer: 8.3 kg ✓

questions.question_text: Round 3.145 litres to the nearest 100 millilitres.
questions.explanation:
3.145 litres = 3 litres and 145 ml.
145 ml to nearest 100 ml: 145 is closer to 100 than to 200 (145 < 150).
Answer: 3.1 litres ✓
questions.working: 3.145 litres → 3.1 litres

questions.question_text: Round 15.83 km to the nearest kilometre.
questions.explanation:
15.83 km: the digit after the decimal is 8 (≥5), so round up.
Answer: 16 km ✓

questions.question_text: Round 247.4 g to the nearest 10 grams.
questions.explanation:
247.4 g to nearest 10 g: look at units digit = 7 (≥5), round up the tens.
240 → round up to 250.
Answer: 250 g ✓

questions.question_text: Round 6.48 m to the nearest 10 centimetres.
questions.explanation:
6.48 m = 6 m and 48 cm.
48 cm to nearest 10 cm: 48 is closer to 50 than to 40 (48 ≥ 45).
Answer: 6.5 m ✓

questions.question_text: Round 19.35 km to the nearest 100 metres.
questions.explanation:
19.35 km = 19 km and 350 m.
350 m to nearest 100 m: 350 is exactly halfway → round up to 400 m = 0.4 km.
Answer: 19.4 km ✓

questions.question_text: Round 0.078 km to the nearest 10 metres.
questions.explanation:
0.078 km = 78 m.
78 m to nearest 10 m: 78 → 80 m = 0.08 km.
Answer: 0.08 km ✓

questions.question_text: Round 5.555 litres to the nearest 100 millilitres.
questions.explanation:
5.555 litres = 5 litres and 555 ml.
555 ml to nearest 100 ml: 555 ≥ 550, so round up to 600 ml = 0.6 litres.
Answer: 5.6 litres ✓
questions.working: 5.555 litres → 5.6 litres

question_banks.skill: Convert weight to units, apply ratio to find daily amount, divide total by daily amount, convert days to weeks.

questions.question_text:
A rabbit weighs 3.0 kg.
The owner buys a bag of rabbit pellets weighing 3.6 kg.

The bag says:
'Give your rabbit 30 g per 500 g body weight per day.'

About how many weeks should the bag last?
questions.explanation:
STEP 1 — Daily food amount:
Rabbit weighs 3000g. Rate = 30g per 500g.
Number of 500g portions: 3000 / 500 = 6
Daily food = 6 x 30 = 180g per day

STEP 2 — Days the bag lasts:
Bag = 3600g. Days = 3600 / 180 = 20 days

STEP 3 — Convert to weeks:
20 days / 7 = approximately 3 weeks ✓
questions.working: Daily = (3000/500) x 30g = 180g/day; Days = 3600/180 = 20 days = about three weeks

question_banks.skill: Know DEFINING properties of kite, rhombus, parallelogram, rectangle, trapezium, equilateral/isosceles/scalene triangle, square, regular hexagon.

questions.question_text:
Consider a kite.

Which property MUST the kite always have?

options.label: C
options.value: Shape has two lines of symmetry

questions.explanation:
A kite MUST have diagonals that intersect at right angles (90 degrees). This is a DEFINING property.

Check each option for kite:
A: Opposite sides parallel — FALSE (kites have NO pairs of parallel sides)
B: Diagonals equal — FALSE (one diagonal is longer than the other)
C: Two lines of symmetry — FALSE (a kite has exactly ONE line of symmetry)
D: Diagonals at right angles — TRUE ✓ (always, for every kite)
E: All angles equal — FALSE (a kite has one pair of equal opposite angles)
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a kite → Answer D

questions.question_text:
Consider a rhombus.

Which property MUST the rhombus always have?
questions.explanation:
A rhombus MUST have all four sides equal — that is its definition.
A: Not necessarily 90 degrees (only if it is a square)
B: Diagonals are equal — FALSE (only equal if it is a square)
C: All sides equal — TRUE ✓
D: Two lines of symmetry — TRUE for a rhombus, but the 'must have' is C (more fundamental)
E: Is a rectangle — FALSE
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a rhombus → Answer C

questions.question_text:
Consider a parallelogram.

Which property MUST the parallelogram always have?

options.label: A
options.value: Opposite sides are parallel and equal

questions.explanation:
A parallelogram MUST have opposite sides parallel AND equal — this is its definition.
B: 90 degree angles — only for rectangles/squares
C: Equal sides — only for rhombuses
D: Diagonals at 90 degrees — only for rhombuses
E: Line of symmetry — FALSE (general parallelogram has NO lines of symmetry)
A: Always true ✓
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a parallelogram → Answer A

questions.question_text:
Consider a rectangle.

Which property MUST the rectangle always have?

options.label: D
options.value: Has exactly one line of symmetry

questions.explanation:
A rectangle MUST have all four angles equal to 90 degrees — that is its definition.
A: Equal sides — only for squares
B: All 90 degree angles — TRUE ✓
C: Diagonals at right angles — FALSE (only for squares among rectangles)
D: Exactly one line of symmetry — FALSE (rectangles have 2 lines of symmetry)
E: Opposite sides unequal — FALSE (opposite sides are EQUAL in a rectangle)
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a rectangle → Answer B

questions.question_text:
Consider a trapezium.

Which property MUST the trapezium always have?

options.label: A
options.value: Both pairs of opposite sides are parallel

options.label: C
options.value: Exactly one pair of parallel sides

questions.explanation:
A trapezium is defined as having EXACTLY ONE pair of parallel sides.
A: Both pairs parallel — that would make it a parallelogram, not a trapezium
B: Equal angles — not required
C: Exactly one pair of parallel sides — TRUE ✓ (the definition)
D: Equal sides — only for special trapeziums
E: Right angles — only for right trapeziums
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a trapezium → Answer C

questions.question_text:
Consider a equilateral triangle.

Which property MUST the equilateral triangle always have?

options.label: A
options.value: Has exactly one line of symmetry

questions.explanation:
An equilateral triangle has all sides equal and all angles equal to 60 degrees.
A: One line of symmetry — FALSE (it has THREE)
B: Right angle — FALSE (all angles are 60 degrees)
C: Is isosceles — TRUE (all equilateral triangles are also isosceles), but E is more specific
D: Obtuse angle — FALSE
E: All angles 60 degrees — TRUE ✓ (the most precise defining property)
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a equilateral triangle → Answer E

questions.question_text:
Consider a isosceles triangle.

Which property MUST the isosceles triangle always have?
questions.explanation:
An isosceles triangle MUST have at least two equal sides.
A: All equal — that is equilateral (a special case)
B: Exactly two equal sides — TRUE ✓ (defining property of isosceles)
C: All angles equal — only for equilateral
D/E: Right/obtuse angles — not required
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a isosceles triangle → Answer B

questions.question_text:
Consider a square.

Which property MUST the square always have?

options.label: A
options.value: Has exactly two lines of symmetry

options.label: D
options.value: All sides equal AND all angles 90 degrees

questions.explanation:
A square MUST have all sides equal AND all angles 90 degrees.
A: Exactly two lines — FALSE (a square has FOUR lines of symmetry)
B: Unequal diagonals — FALSE (square diagonals are equal)
C: Opposite sides parallel only — FALSE (all sides are equal too)
D: All sides equal AND 90 degree angles — TRUE ✓
E: Exactly one right angle — FALSE (all four angles are 90 degrees)
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a square → Answer D

questions.question_text:
Consider a regular hexagon.

Which property MUST the regular hexagon always have?

options.label: A
options.value: Has exactly four lines of symmetry

options.label: C
options.value: All sides equal and all angles equal

questions.explanation:
A regular hexagon MUST have all 6 sides equal AND all 6 angles equal (120 degrees each).
A: Four lines of symmetry — FALSE (regular hexagon has SIX)
B: 90 degree angles — FALSE (120 degrees each)
C: All sides and angles equal — TRUE ✓
D: Diagonals at right angles — not a property of hexagons
E: Quadrilateral — FALSE (hexagon has 6 sides)
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a regular hexagon → Answer C

questions.question_text:
Consider a scalene triangle.

Which property MUST the scalene triangle always have?

options.label: A
options.value: All three sides are different lengths

questions.explanation:
A scalene triangle MUST have all three sides of different lengths.
A: All sides different — TRUE ✓ (definition of scalene)
B: Right angle — not required (could be acute or obtuse scalene)
C: Two equal sides — FALSE (that would make it isosceles)
D: Equal angles — FALSE
E: Obtuse angle — not required
questions.working: Defining/always-true property of a scalene triangle → Answer A
